title: Desktop publishers
rationale: Desktop publishing is almost entirely digital work done on a computer: formatting text, integrating graphics, revising layouts, and preparing files for print or online publication. AI can increasingly automate or accelerate core tasks such as layout generation, proofreading, image editing, and template-based design, likely reducing headcount even where human aesthetic judgment and collaboration still matter.

title: Psychologists
rationale: Psychologists have meaningful exposure because AI can assist with testing, documentation, report writing, research, and some aspects of assessment or treatment planning. But much of the occupation depends on trust, nuanced observation, ethical judgment, licensure, and direct human interaction with clients, students, families, or organizations, which limits full automation of the core role.

title: Marriage and family therapists
rationale: Marriage and family therapists do substantial information-processing work—assessment, treatment planning, documentation, and coordination—where AI can assist and improve productivity. But the core job depends on high-trust human interaction, empathy, nuanced judgment, and live therapeutic relationships, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Cashiers
rationale: Cashiers have significant AI exposure because much of the core job is routine transaction processing that can be automated through self-checkout, computer vision, digital payments, and retail automation systems. The role still requires physical presence and customer interaction, which prevents maximum exposure, but AI and adjacent automation are likely to keep reducing headcount and reshaping the job substantially.

title: Mining and geological engineers
rationale: Mining and geological engineers do substantial analytical and design work on computers—modeling mines, evaluating sites, monitoring production, and writing technical reports—so AI can assist meaningfully with planning, analysis, and documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on site-specific physical conditions, field inspections, safety oversight, and coordination with operations in real mining environments, which limits full automation.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rationale: AI exposure is very high overall because film and video editing is almost entirely digital, and AI can already assist with organizing footage, rough cuts, color/audio cleanup, effects, and content generation. Exposure is somewhat tempered by camera-operator work, which still requires physical presence, equipment handling, and creative on-set judgment, but robotic cameras and workflow automation will still reduce labor needs and reshape the occupation.

title: Special effects artists and animators
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly digital: creating graphics, animation, visual effects, and sometimes code on a computer, often even from home. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, style transfer, rotoscoping, motion, and other core production tasks, so it is likely to significantly reshape workflows and reduce labor needed for many routine or iterative parts, even though human creativity, taste, and collaboration still matter.

title: Set and exhibit designers

title: Genetic counselors
rationale: Genetic counselors do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: collecting histories, reviewing records, interpreting test results, researching conditions, and drafting documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on high-stakes clinical judgment, patient-specific communication, coordination with providers, and emotional support for distressed clients, which limits full automation.

title: Graphic designers
rationale: Graphic design is overwhelmingly digital work: creating layouts, images, and visual concepts on software, often entirely from a computer. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, layout iteration, branding concepts, and production tasks, so it can both automate portions of the work and greatly increase each designer’s output, though client interaction, taste, and higher-level creative direction still keep exposure just below the maximum.

title: Heavy and tractor-trailer truck drivers
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and happens in the real world, requiring on-road vehicle control, cargo inspection, securing loads, and handling unpredictable traffic and weather. That limits near-term AI automation compared with fully digital jobs, but autonomous-driving systems, route optimization, and automated logging/dispatch could still significantly reshape long-haul trucking and reduce labor demand over time.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
rationale: This occupation’s core output is fully digital text generated from speech, and AI speech recognition, real-time transcription, and captioning tools are improving rapidly and already automate large portions of the work. Human workers still matter for legal-grade accuracy, speaker identification, clarification, and quality control in high-stakes settings, but the occupation is likely to be heavily reshaped by AI through automation and reduced labor needed per transcript.

title: Judges and hearing officers
rationale: Judges and hearing officers do substantial knowledge work that AI can increasingly assist with: researching legal issues, reviewing briefs, summarizing records, and drafting opinions. But the core role also depends on human authority, procedural oversight, credibility judgments, and in-person courtroom or hearing management, which creates a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Computer support specialists
rationale: Computer support specialists do predominantly digital work—diagnosing issues, guiding users, documenting problems, and troubleshooting systems over phone, chat, email, or remote tools—so AI can automate or heavily assist much of the core workflow. Some exposure is limited by the need for hands-on device setup, onsite support, and complex real-time customer interaction, but the occupation is already being reshaped by chatbots and automated troubleshooting.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
rationale: This job is largely digital and computer-based: organizing clinical databases, validating records, generating reports, analyzing outcomes, and supporting health information systems are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Human oversight remains important because of healthcare regulations, patient privacy, data integrity, and coordination with clinical staff, but AI is likely to significantly raise productivity and reshape the role.

title: Property, real estate, and community association managers
rationale: This job mixes sizable administrative and digital work—leasing communications, recordkeeping, budgeting, reporting, marketing vacancies, and maintenance coordination—that AI can increasingly automate or streamline. But much of the core role still depends on physical presence, property inspections, showing units, contractor oversight, and handling tenant or homeowner disputes, which limits full automation.

title: Delivery truck drivers and driver/sales workers
rationale: This job is centered on physical driving, loading/unloading, and in-person delivery in unpredictable real-world environments, which limits near-term AI automation. But AI can already optimize routing, dispatching, customer communication, and paperwork, and over time advances in autonomous vehicles and delivery systems could significantly reshape staffing and productivity.

title: Compliance officers
rationale: Compliance officers do predominantly computer-based knowledge work: reading regulations, analyzing risks, auditing records, writing policies and reports, and answering compliance questions. AI is well suited to summarizing rules, monitoring documents, drafting guidance, and flagging issues, though human judgment, accountability, investigations, and stakeholder interaction keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Customer service representatives
rationale: This job is highly exposed because much of the core work—answering questions, handling routine requests, processing account changes, documenting interactions, and communicating by phone/chat/email—is digital and increasingly automatable by AI. Some roles still require human judgment, empathy, or in-person help for complex and upset customers, but the occupation is already being reduced by self-service systems and AI will likely further shrink headcount by handling a growing share of standard interactions.

title: Air traffic controllers
rationale: Air traffic control involves substantial information processing on screens, and AI can assist with routing, conflict detection, weather integration, and decision support—potentially letting each controller manage more traffic. But the core job is real-time, safety-critical control of aircraft in the physical world under heavy regulation, where human judgment, accountability, and rapid coordination remain major barriers to full automation.

title: Speech-language pathologists
rationale: Speech-language pathologists do substantial assessment, documentation, and treatment planning that AI can assist with, and AI tools may increasingly support screening, progress notes, and therapy materials. title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
rationale: This job’s core work happens in person, in real time, on the field or court, requiring physical presence, positioning, visual observation, and split-second judgment under pressure. AI may assist at the margins through video replay, training, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the core officiating role broadly in the near term.

title: Writers and authors
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: researching, drafting, revising, and producing text can largely be done from a computer anywhere. AI is rapidly improving at core writing tasks such as generating copy, summarizing research, adapting tone, and iterating drafts, so it is likely to both automate some work directly and sharply increase output per writer, even though top-tier creativity, original reporting, and client/audience judgment still preserve some human role.

title: Photographers
rationale: Photography has meaningful AI exposure because image editing, enhancement, archiving, marketing, and some commercial image creation are digital tasks that AI can increasingly automate or compress. But the occupation still relies heavily on physical presence, equipment handling, real-world image capture, and client interaction—especially for weddings, portraits, events, and on-location shoots—so AI is more likely to reshape parts of the job than fully replace it.

title: Environmental engineers
rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial computer-based knowledge work—analyzing data, preparing reports, reviewing permits, and designing pollution-control or water-treatment systems—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on site inspections, project monitoring, regulatory accountability, and engineering judgment tied to physical infrastructure, which limits full automation.
